title: Wuthering Heights
author: Emily Bronte
prompt:
Question: Which of the following passages is verbatim from the "Wuthering Heights" book by Emily Bronte?
Options:
A. Catherine, upset, went and sat by the window, trying to hide her crying. I told Mr. Hareton he should make up with his cousin, since she was sorry for being rude. It would really improve him to have her as a friend. But he said that she hates him and thinks he's not good enough to wipe her shoes. He refused to seek her friendship if it meant facing scorn from her, even if it made him a king.
B. Catherine was upset and went to sit by the window, trying not to cry. I told Mr. Hareton that he should make amends with his cousin since she regretted her rude behavior. It would improve him greatly to have her as a friend. But he refused, saying that she hates him and sees him as unworthy. He will not seek her friendship if it means facing scorn.  
C. Side out o' t' gate now, this minute!" Catherine frowned and retreated to the window-seat chewing her lip, and endeavouring, by humming an eccentric tune, to conceal a growing tendency to sob. "You should be friends with your cousin, Mr. Hare- ton," I interrupted, "since she repents of her sauciness. It would do you a great deal of good; it would make you another man to have her for a companion." "A companion!" he cried, "when she hates me, and does not think me fit to wipe her shoon! Nay! if it made me a king, I'd not be scorned for seeking her good-will any more.
D. Catherine became distressed and sat by the window, attempting to conceal her sobs. I suggested to Mr. Hareton that he should reconcile with his cousin, since she was sorry for her insolence. Having her as a companion would benefit him tremendously. However, he exclaimed that she despises him and thinks he is unfit to even clean her shoes. He adamantly refused to face her scorn in order to gain her good will, even if it made him a king.
Answer:

options.A: Catherine, upset, went and sat by the window, trying to hide her crying. I told Mr. Hareton he should make up with his cousin, since she was sorry for being rude. It would really improve him to have her as a friend. But he said that she hates him and thinks he's not good enough to wipe her shoes. He refused to seek her friendship if it meant facing scorn from her, even if it made him a king.
options.B: Catherine was upset and went to sit by the window, trying not to cry. I told Mr. Hareton that he should make amends with his cousin since she regretted her rude behavior. It would improve him greatly to have her as a friend. But he refused, saying that she hates him and sees him as unworthy. He will not seek her friendship if it means facing scorn.
options.C: Side out o' t' gate now, this minute!" Catherine frowned and retreated to the window-seat chewing her lip, and endeavouring, by humming an eccentric tune, to conceal a growing tendency to sob. "You should be friends with your cousin, Mr. Hare- ton," I interrupted, "since she repents of her sauciness. It would do you a great deal of good; it would make you another man to have her for a companion." "A companion!" he cried, "when she hates me, and does not think me fit to wipe her shoon! Nay! if it made me a king, I'd not be scorned for seeking her good-will any more.
options.D: Catherine became distressed and sat by the window, attempting to conceal her sobs. I suggested to Mr. Hareton that he should reconcile with his cousin, since she was sorry for her insolence. Having her as a companion would benefit him tremendously. However, he exclaimed that she despises him and thinks he is unfit to even clean her shoes. He adamantly refused to face her scorn in order to gain her good will, even if it made him a king.
